Name: No. 1 Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's June 1940 directive for raiding forces after Dunkirk
Key Individuals: Lt Col Dudley Clarke (concept originator), Churchill
Institutionalization: Disbanded; legacy in 3 Commando Brigade RM

Name: No. 2 Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's directive; original formation for parachute role; redesignated 11th SAS Bn Nov 1940; second No. 2 reformed from 1st SS Bn
Key Individuals: Lt Col A.C. Newman VC (CO, St Nazaire), Lt Col Jack Churchill
Institutionalization: Original No. 2 became the Parachute Regiment (Aug 1942). Second No. 2 disbanded post-war

Name: No. 3 Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's June 1940 directive
Key Individuals: Brig Peter Young DSO MC (notable CO)
Institutionalization: Disbanded; legacy in RM Commandos

Name: No. 4 Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's June 1940 directive
Institutionalization: Disbanded; legacy in RM Commandos

Name: No. 5 Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's June 1940 directive
Institutionalization: Disbanded

Name: No. 6 Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's June 1940 directive
Institutionalization: Disbanded

Name: No. 7 Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's directive; sent to ME as 'A' Bn Layforce
Institutionalization: Disbanded with Layforce. Personnel fed SAS, SBS, LRDG

Name: No. 8 (Guards) Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's directive; raised from Brigade of Guards
Key Individuals: Robert Laycock; David Stirling, Randolph Churchill, George Jellicoe (members)
Institutionalization: Disbanded. Personnel founded SAS (Stirling), SBS (Jellicoe)

Name: No. 9 Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's June 1940 directive
Institutionalization: Disbanded

Name: No. 11 (Scottish) Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's directive; sent to ME as 'C' Bn Layforce
Key Individuals: Lt Col Geoffrey Keyes VC (killed in Rommel HQ raid, Nov 1941)
Institutionalization: Disbanded with Layforce

Name: No. 12 Commando
Trigger of Formation: Churchill's directive; maintained raiding role
Institutionalization: Disbanded late 1943 to supply reinforcements

Name: Combined Operations Headquarters (COHQ)
Trigger of Formation: Need for centralized tri-service amphibious/raiding planning after Fall of France
Key Individuals: Adm Roger Keyes (1940–41), Lord Mountbatten (1941–43), Maj Gen Laycock (1943–47)
Institutionalization: Legacy in NATO joint ops doctrine; UK amphibious warfare HQ

Name: Special Service Brigade & Signals Troops
Trigger of Formation: Need to organize growing Commando units under centralized command
Key Individuals: Brig J.C. Haydon (first CO); redesignated Commando Brigades Dec 1944
Institutionalization: 3 Commando Brigade RM – active today

Name: Independent Companies and Special Service Battalions
Trigger of Formation: German invasion of Norway (Apr 1940); need for guerrilla raiding force
Key Individuals: Col Colin Gubbins (MI(R)); Maj Gen R.H. Dewing
Institutionalization: Direct precursors to Commandos. Gubbins later led SOE

Name: No. 51 (Middle East) Commando
Tags: Commando, ME, East Africa, Palestinian volunteers
Trigger of Formation: Decision to form Commandos in Egypt for ME operations
Key Individuals: Lt Col Henry J. Cator MC (CO); ~300 Palestinian Jewish/Arab volunteers
Institutionalization: Absorbed into ME Commando. Veterans joined SIG and other units

Name: Special Operations Executive (SOE)
Trigger of Formation: Fall of France; Churchill's directive to 'set Europe ablaze'
Key Individuals: Hugh Dalton (Minister), Sir Frank Nelson (1st CD), Brig Colin Gubbins (Dir Ops/CD), Gladwyn Jebb
Institutionalization: 280 personnel absorbed into MI6. Influenced CIA creation via OSS partnership

Name: Long Range Desert Group (LRDG)
Tags: Desert, Recon, Long Range Patrol, LRDG
Date Formed: June 1940 (as Long Range Patrol; renamed Nov 1940)
Trigger of Formation: Need for deep penetration reconnaissance in Western Desert; intelligence gap in Libya
Key Individuals: Maj Ralph A. Bagnold (founder); Gen Wavell (authorized); predominantly NZ volunteers initially
Institutionalization: No direct successor. Influenced post-war SAS and long-range recon doctrine

Name: Special Boat Section/Squadron (SBS)
Tags: Naval SF, Canoe, Frogmen, SBS, Commando
Trigger of Formation: Capt Roger Courtney proved covert canoe infiltration by boarding HMS Glengyle
Key Individuals: Capt Roger 'Jumbo' Courtney (founder); Lord Jellicoe (independent SBS CO from 1943)
Institutionalization: Special Boat Service – active today (RM; renamed 1987)

Name: Auxiliary Units
Trigger of Formation: Threat of German invasion of Britain post-Dunkirk; Churchill ordered formation
Key Individuals: Col Colin Gubbins (organizer); Capt Peter Fleming; trained at Coleshill House
Institutionalization: Never activated. Many members later recruited into SAS and SOE

Name: MI9
Trigger of Formation: Need to facilitate POW escapes and debrief returning personnel for intelligence
Key Individuals: Brig Norman Crockatt DSO MC (founder/head); Airey Neave; Christopher Clayton Hutton (escape gadgets)
Institutionalization: Successor: Reserve Reconnaissance Unit → 23 SAS Regiment

Name: No. 50 (ME) Commando
Trigger of Formation: Raised locally in Egypt for ME operations
Institutionalization: Merged into 'D' Bn Layforce; disbanded

Name: No. 52 (ME) Commando
Trigger of Formation: Raised locally in Egypt for ME operations
Institutionalization: Merged into 'D' Bn Layforce; disbanded
